content.problemStatement:
You are given two integers NNN and XXX.

Count the number of distinct arrays of length **at most** NNN, whose LCM is equal to XXX.

That is, find the number of arrays AAA such that:


1≤∣A∣≤N1 \leq |A| \leq N1≤∣A∣≤N, where ∣A∣|A|∣A∣ denotes the length of AAA; and
lcm(A1,A2,A3,…,A∣A∣)=X\text{lcm}(A_1, A_2, A_3, \ldots, A_{|A|}) = Xlcm(A1​,A2​,A3​,…,A∣A∣​)=X
Two arrays AAA and BBB are considered different if:


AAA and BBB have different lengths; or
AAA and BBB have the same length, but Ai≠BiA_i \neq B_iAi​=Bi​ for some index 1≤i≤∣A∣1 \leq i \leq |A|1≤i≤∣A∣.
The answer may be large, so compute it modulo 998244353998244353998244353.


Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
Each test case consists of a single line containing two space-separated integers NNN and XXX, the parameters specified in the statement.


Output Format

For each test case, output on a new line the number of possible arrays with length at most NNN and LCM equal to XXX, modulo 998244353998244353998244353.
constraints: The sum of NNN over all test cases won't exceed 2⋅1052 \cdot 10^52⋅105.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Test case 111: We're looking for arrays of length at most 222 and whose LCM is 333.
The only such arrays are [3],[1,3],[3,1],[3,3][3], [1, 3], [3, 1], [3, 3][3],[1,3],[3,1],[3,3].
